description: Human-flagged claims missed or needed from calibration of Prologue and Chapter 1 of Why Knowledge Matters. Each item is a claim/concept the human reviewer identified as important but the pipeline missed or should capture.

items.text: The achievement gap between social groups can be ameliorated by knowledge-based schooling.
items.notes: Major thesis of the book, rich and citable

items.text: The French national curriculum focused on imparting the same knowledge to all students, and this approach was successful in equalizing educational outcomes.
items.notes: Historical claim about France's pre-reform success

items.text: French education schools were subjected to 20 years of indoctrination in American progressive educational ideas before the 1989 reform.
items.notes: Causal mechanism claim — how the reform was prepared

items.text: The French reform of 1989 constituted a wholesale adoption of American-style individualized, skill-centered education — Hirsch's characterization of this as 'Americanization' is itself an interpretive claim.
items.notes: Meta-level: the author's narrative framing is a claim

items.text: After the French curriculum reform, there was a steep decline in academic achievement across all demographic groups, not just disadvantaged students.
items.notes: Empirical claim about breadth of decline

items.text: American academic scores fell rapidly at all grade levels between 1960 and 1980.
items.notes: Historical/empirical factual premise

items.text: Three specific ideas — naturalism, individualism, and skill-centrism — caused the American educational decline.
items.notes: Causal claim linking specific ideas to outcomes

items.text: Whole nations have successfully achieved high and equitable educational outcomes by following communal knowledge curricula delivered through national curricula.
items.notes: Cross-national empirical generalization

items.text: Educational individualism has always required the general-skills idea to maintain coherence, because without a shared curriculum the only unifying concept is transferable skills.
items.notes: Theoretical dependency claim

items.text: Dewey's central claim that critical thinking is a unifying, teachable skill was debunked by subsequent cognitive science.
items.notes: Historical/empirical claim about intellectual history

items.text: Cognitive skills are domain-specific: expertise in one domain does not transfer to proficiency in another.
items.notes: Core theoretical claim from cognitive science

items.text: Developmental appropriateness — the idea that children can only learn certain things at certain ages — lacks scientific basis and has been used to justify withholding academic content from young children.
items.notes: Contested concept that Hirsch defines and attacks

items.text: Benign providence — the Romantic idea that nature will guide children's development if adults step back — is a foundational assumption of naturalistic education that Hirsch rejects.
items.notes: Concept underlying naturalism component

items.text: The primary goal of early education should be to impart shared communal knowledge that enables participation in the public sphere, not individual self-realization.
items.notes: Normative/prescriptive claim about education's purpose

items.text: Tests should be based on well-defined, knowledge-based curriculums — this is the only way to make tests fair, productive, and also fewer and better.
items.notes: Prescriptive claim — 'fewer and better' part was missed
items.selected_text: There's just one way to do that—to base them on well-defined, knowledge-based curriculums. There is no other way of making tests fair and productive

items.text: A valid reading test probes a student's degree of initiation into the public sphere, which is a fundamental aim of education.
items.notes: First of two claims from one sentence — linking reading tests to civic purpose
items.selected_text: Since a valid reading test probes a student's degree of initiation into the public sphere—a fundamental aim of education—any policy that lowers or neglects to improve test scores in reading is a failed educational policy.

items.text: Any educational policy that lowers or neglects to improve reading test scores is a failed educational policy.
items.notes: Second of two claims — the normative conclusion. Compound claim needs splitting.
items.selected_text: Since a valid reading test probes a student's degree of initiation into the public sphere—a fundamental aim of education—any policy that lowers or neglects to improve test scores in reading is a failed educational policy.

items.text: In math, American scores for seventeen-year-olds have been stable for many years, in contrast to declining reading scores.
items.notes: Factual premise — math vs reading divergence is analytically important
items.selected_text: In math, in contrast to reading, American scores for seventeen- year-olds have been stable for many years.

items.text: If one had to choose a single measure of educational quality, reading scores of seventeen-year-olds would be the best choice.
items.notes: Extremely important, load-bearing claim with much disagreement
items.selected_text: If one had to choose a single measure...

items.text: The testing regimens introduced by NCLB have clearly helped improve the mechanics of early reading.
items.notes: Concession — Hirsch grants this before arguing it's insufficient
items.selected_text: the testing regimens have clearly helped improve the mechanics of early reading

items.text: Younger students can now decode texts with more fluency and accuracy than they did before NCLB.
items.notes: Empirical factual claim, interesting given reading wars and phonics discussion
items.selected_text: Younger students can now decode texts with more fluency and accuracy than they did be- fore NCLB.

items.text: There is no reliable main-idea-finding skill that can substitute for content knowledge in reading comprehension.
items.notes: Direct theoretical claim
items.selected_text: The no- tion that skill in finding the main idea can take the place of content is worse than empty; it's actively misleading. There is no reliable main-idea-finding skill.

items.text: The utility of reading comprehension strategy instruction ceases after about ten lessons; two weeks of strategy instruction is optimal.
items.notes: Specific empirical claim with scholarly discussion around it
items.selected_text: Their utility ceases after about ten lessons. Two weeks on comprehension strategies is optimal.

items.text: Once decoding has been mastered and fluency attained, relevant background knowledge becomes the chief component of reading skill.
items.notes: Core theoretical mechanism claim
items.selected_text: But once decoding has been mastered and fluency attained, relevant knowledge becomes the chief component of reading skill.
